title: Woodbury County Courthouse

infobox.value: 620 Douglas Street [Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa")

[George Grant Elmslie](/wiki/George_Grant_Elmslie "George Grant Elmslie"),  [William L. Steele](/wiki/William_L._Steele "William L. Steele"),  [William Gray Purcell](/wiki/William_Gray_Purcell "William Gray Purcell")

is located at 620 Douglas Street in [Sioux City](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"), the county seat of [Woodbury County, Iowa](/wiki/Woodbury_County,_Iowa "Woodbury County, Iowa"), United States. It is regarded as "one of the finest [Prairie School](/wiki/Prairie_School "Prairie School") buildings in the United States"[[3]](#cite_note-nrhpinv2-3) and has been declared a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") for its architecture. It is used for legal proceedings in the county.

["National Register Information System"](https://npgallery.nps.gov/NRHP). *[National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places")*. [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"). January 23, 2007.

The **Woodbury County Courthouse** is located at 620 Douglas Street in [Sioux City](/wiki/Sioux_City,_Iowa "Sioux City, Iowa"), the county seat of [Woodbury County, Iowa](/wiki/Woodbury_County,_Iowa "Woodbury County, Iowa"), United States. It is regarded as "one of the finest [Prairie School](/wiki/Prairie_School "Prairie School") buildings in the United States"[[3]](#cite_note-nrhpinv2-3) and has been declared a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") for its architecture. It is used for legal proceedings in the county.

## Early courthouses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodbury_County_Courthouse&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early courthouses")]

Initially, crude log structures were used for county business.[[4]](#cite_note-Stanek-4) Sioux City was made the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") in 1856, and it was at this time a county-owned courthouse was considered. It was to be located on the public square and the foundation was laid in 1857. The contract to complete the building was let two years later, but it was canceled before construction could begin. County offices continued to be located in various locations in the city.

Voters approved the construction of a courthouse in October 1875. It was designed by Des Moines architect [William L. Foster](/wiki/William_Foster_(Iowa_architect) "William Foster (Iowa architect)") and built by brothers Charles E. Hedges and Daniel T. Hedges for $75,000. Designed in the [Second Empire style](/wiki/Second_Empire_style "Second Empire style"), the building was composed of [Kasota limestone](/wiki/Kasota_limestone "Kasota limestone"). It featured a [mansard roof](/wiki/Mansard_roof "Mansard roof") with iron [cresting](/wiki/Cresting_(architecture) "Cresting (architecture)") and a corner tower capped with a dome and a statue of [Lady Justice](/wiki/Lady_Justice "Lady Justice").[[4]](#cite_note-Stanek-4)

## Present courthouse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Woodbury_County_Courthouse&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Present courthouse")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Woodbury-interior2.jpg/250px-Woodbury-interior2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woodbury-interior2.jpg)

Courthouse interior

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Elevator_Door_Piece.jpg/250px-Elevator_Door_Piece.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elevator_Door_Piece.jpg)

Elevator Door Panel produced by Crown Iron Works

In 1914, the county determined that the courthouse was too small for the city and they decided to build a new one. The old courthouse was sold and the new one, designed by the Minneapolis architect [George Grant Elmslie](/wiki/George_Grant_Elmslie "George Grant Elmslie") in collaboration with the Sioux City architect [William L. Steele](/wiki/William_L._Steele "William L. Steele") and Elmslie's partner, [William Gray Purcell](/wiki/William_Gray_Purcell "William Gray Purcell"), was constructed from July 10, 1916 to March 1, 1918 at a cost of $850,000. This building is the current courthouse, located at the southeast corner of Douglas and Seventh Streets. It is a nearly square four story structure, built out of [Roman brick](/wiki/Roman_brick "Roman brick"), with [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and terra cotta trim elements. It has a 157-foot (48 m) eight-story tower, and features sculptural work by [Alfonso Iannelli](/wiki/Alfonso_Iannelli "Alfonso Iannelli") over its doors. The northern entrance features metal grillwork designed by Elmslie. The interior floors are marble, and the central rotunda features a glass mosaic drinking fountain in one wall. All ornamental metal work was produced by Crown Iron Works of Minneapolis, Minnesota. The interior murals were painted by John Norton of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[3]](#cite_note-nrhpinv2-3)

In 1973, the building was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). It was declared a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") in 1996.[[2]](#cite_note-nhlsum-2)[[3]](#cite_note-nrhpinv2-3)
